subject: Sports
title: Terminal Velocity in Skydiving
description: One-minute skill: Terminal Velocity in Skydiving.

hook.text: A skydiver jumps from 15,000 feet. After about 12 seconds of freefall, they stop accelerating.

buildup.text: Gravity pulls them down, but air resistance pushes back up. These forces eventually balance out.

discovery.text: That balance point is terminal velocity—about 120 mph for a spread-eagle skydiver.

twist.text: A head-down diver reduces air resistance and reaches 180 mph. Body position controls speed, not weight.

climax.text: Felix Baumgartner jumped from the stratosphere in 2012 and broke the speed of sound—843 mph in thin air.

punchline.text: Falling speed has a limit. Unless you jump from space.

quiz.question: What determines a skydiver's terminal velocity?
options: The balance between gravity and air resistance
